coredata.dc:title: Dual-functional membranes of CQDs/TiO2 @PI/PES nanofibers for the high filter efficiency of ultrafine particles and photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hua-Wei
Kuo, Yu-Lin
Huang, Shu-Hsien
Chiou, Chyow-San
Chen, Ying-Cih
Chen, Wei-Ting
coredata.dc:description: The carbon quantum dot/titanium dioxide (CQDs/TiO2), fabricated on highly porous polyimide (PI)/polyether sulfone (PES) nanofiber via electrospinning process, called the dual-functional CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane, was developed and evaluated for its performance, including both removal of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and ultrafine particulate matters (PM0.1). The photocatalytic activity of synthesized CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membranes with 5% CQD loading on the decomposition of acetone was significantly enhanced compared with that of TiO2(P25)@PI/PES. The comprehensive results of photoluminescence emission, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and photocatalytic experiments suggest that up-conversion properties of CQD can enhance the absorption of visible light on TiO2 and reduce the reorganization of electron-hole pairs. Further, the optimized CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane could effectively photodegrade of acetone (>94.5%) and also maintained a good PM0.1 filtration efficiency (> 99%) and a differential pressure of 0.34 Pa for creating level 3 medical face masks as per the standards of ASTM F2299 and EN 14683. Therefore, the dual-functional CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane could be applied as a promising photocatalytic filter for air purification that simultaneously and remarkably filters PM0.1 and decompose VOCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Electrospinning
Filiation
Photocatalytic
Ultrafine particulate
Volatile organic compounds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characteristics of CQD, CQD/TiO2, and CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membranes
Crystal phase analysis via X-ray diffractometer
Crystal phases analysis via X-ray diffraction spectra
Results of photocatalytic degradation at different experimental conditions by the Taguchi method
Factor influencing the content of PI/PES on the fiber diameter, viscosity, thickness, and photocatalytic degradation of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane
Effect of CQD/TiO2 contents on photocatalytic degradation and filter efficiency
Effect of carbon quantum dot loading on photocatalytic degradation
Effect of light wavelength on photocatalytic degradation of acetone
Kinetic calculation of CQD loading on photocatalytic degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of annular photoreactor and filter testing system.

Morphology of synthesized CQD, CQD/TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES: (A) CQD solution was emitted without and with UV light; (B) synthesized CQD by TEM; (C) CQD/TiO2 by TEM; (D) elemental mapping and distribution of Ti; (E) elemental mapping and distribution of C.
XPS spectra of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 (A) C 1 s; (B) Ti 2p; (C) O 1 s.
(A) PL emission spectra of CQD from 290 to 440 nm; (B) PL emission spectra of CQD from 600 to 800 nm; (C) PL emission spectra of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 at 310 nm; (D) Photocatalytic mechanism of CQD/TiO2.
Morphology of 5 wt% CQD/TiO2 on various content of PI/PES: (A) 36 wt% PI/PES (B) 38 wt% PI/PES (C) 40 wt% PI/PES (D) 42 wt% PI/PES (E) 42 wt% PI/PES after filtration of NaCl particles.
Effect of CQD/TiO2 contents on penetration efficiency.
Effect of CQD loading on photocatalytic degradation of acetone.
Effect of light wavelength on photocatalytic degradation of acetone.
(A)Effect of light intensity on the photodegradation of acetone; (B)Reaction order of light intensity for photodegradation of acetone.
The recycled test for photocatalytic degradation of acetone. *The degradation value equaled to 83% at time of 5 hr and then changed to 74% & 73% at the time of 11 hr and 17 hr respectively.

Photocatalytic degradation of Isopropyl Alcohol (IPA) at the concentration of 150 ppm, *Approximate 83% of IPA could be degraded via the irradiation at wavelength of 365 nm while the IPA concentration was 150 ppm with 5 hrs operation.

Ultraviolet/visible spectrum of different materials, *The intensity of CQD/TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES were higher than the value of TiO2 at wavelength ranged as exceeded 400 nm. Therefore, the addition of CQD was benefit to photocatalytic degradation at wavelength of visible light (over 400 nm).
Experimental results and S/N values of photocatalytic degradation of 300 ppm acetone at different operational conditions.
Factor influence order analysis of four experimental factors.
Effect of PI/PES on photocatalytic degradation and filter efficiency with 5 wt% QD/TiO2 filter at 20 kV and collection time of 6 h.
Various contents of CQD/TiO2 on photocatalytic degradation and filter efficiency with 5 wt% CQD loading at 20 kV and collection time of 6 h.
Comparison of photocatalytic degradation, particle filtration efficiency, differential pressure, and filtration quality at various filter materials.

head.ce:title: Dual-functional membranes of CQDs/TiO2 @PI/PES nanofibers for the high filter efficiency of ultrafine particles and photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Ilan University, Yilan 260, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Ilan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Cosmetic Application & Management, St. Mary’s Junior College of Medicine, Nursing and Management, Yilan 266, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Cosmetic Application & Management, St. Mary’s Junior College of Medicine, Nursing and Management

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Cosmetic Application & Management, St. Mary’s Junior College of Medicine, Nursing and Management, Yilan 266, Taiwan, ROC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon quantum dot/titanium dioxide (CQDs/TiO2), fabricated on highly porous polyimide (PI)/polyether sulfone (PES) nanofiber via electrospinning process, called the dual-functional CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane, was developed and evaluated for its performance, including both removal of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and ultrafine particulate matters (PM0.1). The photocatalytic activity of synthesized CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membranes with 5% CQD loading on the decomposition of acetone was significantly enhanced compared with that of TiO2(P25)@PI/PES. The comprehensive results of photoluminescence emission, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and photocatalytic experiments suggest that up-conversion properties of CQD can enhance the absorption of visible light on TiO2 and reduce the reorganization of electron-hole pairs. Further, the optimized CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane could effectively photodegrade of acetone (>94.5%) and also maintained a good PM0.1 filtration efficiency (> 99%) and a differential pressure of 0.34 Pa for creating level 3 medical face masks as per the standards of ASTM F2299 and EN 14683. Therefore, the dual-functional CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane could be applied as a promising photocatalytic filter for air purification that simultaneously and remarkably filters PM0.1 and decompose VOCs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Air pollution is a major global environmental risk to our health and food security because of serious human and natural environmental threats. the dual-functional CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane could be applied as a promising photocatalytic filter for air purification that simultaneously and remarkably filters PM0.1 and decompose VOCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Air pollution is a major global environmental risk to our health and food security because of serious human and natural environmental threats [1–3]. According to Air Pollution - World Health Organization (WHO), the combined effects of ambient and household air pollution are associated with 7 million premature deaths annually. Particulate matter (PM) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are two major causes of air pollution, and most sources of two air pollution come from anthropogenic activities, which dramatically influence air quality. Long-term health effects from air pollution include heart disease, asthmatic syndrome, respiratory diseases [4–6], cardiovascular disease [4–6], and lung cancer [7–11].

In various photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most common chemical used in photocatalysis reactions due to its favorable physical/chemical properties, simple synthesis process, high stability, low cost, and high efficiency in eliminating air pollution. However, it is difficult to activate under visible light due to its band gap of 3.2 V [12]. Doping TiO2 with atoms (silver [13], N [14]), carbon compounds (carbon nanotube [15], graphene [16], quantum dots [16,17], g-C3N4 [18]), metal oxide (Bi2O4 [19], and MoO3 [20]) has been synthetically improved to inhibit the fast charge pair recombination and also enhance the visible light absorption. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of zero-dimension carbon materials with a size below 10 nm for enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 active sites [17]. Moreover, CQDs acquired great optical and electronic characters, involved of light capture, electron harvest, flexible photoluminescence, and especially excellent photoluminescence ability. Besides, multi photon passed through CQDs induced the energy of light transformed from low to high value. CQDs have attracted much attention in drug delivery and bioimaging fields due to their high stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, wide light absorption width, and low cost [21].

In the above-discussed background, there are some eliminations for PM, and VOSs in polluted air. These methods include filtration, photocatalysis, biological treatment, incineration, and adsorption to acquire high efficiency, low cost, and rigid stabilization on treatments of air pollution [22,23]. Nanofiber membranes, as filtration usage, have the advantages of changeable appearance, adjustable fiber diameter, high porosity, alterable composition addition, and large specific surface area for filtering air pollutants [22]. One feasible solution for using powder TiO2 as a photocatalysis application and a nanofiber membrane as a filter is by attaching powder TiO2 on a nanofiber membrane (carrier). However, a limited number of studies related to the photocatalytic properties of CQD deposited on the surface of TiO2 and the filtration mechanism of CQD/TiO2 nanofiber membrane for both treatment of VOCs and PM.

This study investigates the influence of CQD/TiO2 characteristics, the temporal degradation behaviors of acetone under various wavelengths of light illumination, filtration mechanisms of nanofiber membrane, and electrospinning parameters (contents of CQD/TiO2, operational voltage, and electrospinning time). This research attempted to produce CQD/TiO2 nanofiber membranes (CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES) by mixing with CQD/TiO2 and polyimide (PI)/polyether sulfone (PES) through the electrospinning process. The Taguchi method was used to optimally produce CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membranes with high filter efficiency, nanosized diameter, low airflow resistance, small-sized fiber aperture, and high photocatalytic activity.

This study used reagent-grade titanium tetrabutanolate, acetic acid, citric acid, sodium hydroxide, ethanol, polyimide (PI), polyether sulfone (PES), dimethylacetamide (DMAc), and sodium chloride (NaCl) as samples from Alfa Chemistry, BASF Taiwan Ltd., and Acros Organics.

To prepare CQDs by hydrothermal method, 40 g citric acid (carbon precursors) was dissolved in a mixture of 60% water and 40% ethanol and heated at 180 ℃ for 40 h in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal synthesis autoclave reactor. Subsequently, the resulting brown gel was gradually dissolved in an aqueous NaOH solution of NaOH (5 M, pH 7). Finally, CQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying procedure.

Five hundred milliliters of aqueous solution containing 9.7 wt% and 17.0 wt% of acetic acid and titanium tetrabutanolate (TBOT) were held in a 1000 mL beaker and was then hydrolyzed for 2 days with magnetic stirring. The aqueous solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal synthesis autoclave reactor and heated at 200 ℃ for 4 h. The TiO2 nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation and washed five times with absolute alcohol successively.

Acetic acid (9.7 wt%) was dissolved in 73.3 wt% deionized water, and the aqueous solution was then mixed and hydrolyzed with 17 wt% TBOT for 3 days. Predetermined amount (0, 3, 5, and 7 wt%) of CQD in 5 mL deionized water; the aqueous solution was mixed with 15 mL of ethanol containing TiO2 for 2 h with magnetic stirring. The mixed solution was then placed in a hydrothermal autoclave reactor and the temperature was maintained at 200 ℃ for 4 h. CQD/TiO2 powders were produced after centrifugation with absolute alcohol and air-dried at 70 ℃.

Fig. 1 presents a typical installation of the electrospinning process, which involves some operating variables, such as flow rate, applied voltage, spinning time, and distance between the collector plate and spray nozzle. Using high DC voltage, the nozzle sprays polymer solution formed a nano-meter-sized membrane on a collector plate, with the distance of 15 cm between the collector plate and the 21-gauge needle of a 10 mL syringe at the temperature of 24.5 to 27.5 ℃ and relative humidity of 45% to 50%. Predetermined amounts (3, 5, and 7 wt%) of CQD/TiO2 were dispersed in dimethylacetamide (DMAC) under sonication illumination for one hour. Polyimide (PI)/polyethersulfone (PES) polymer solution (38, 40, and 42 wt%) with the PI and PES ratio of 3:7 was dissolved in DMAC and stirred at 50 ℃ for 8 h. An FES-COS electrospinning apparatus (Falco Co, Taiwan) was used to obtain the CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES composite membranes by electrospinning the mixed PI/PES polymer and CQD/TiO2 solutions.

A transformation infrared spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Spectrum 100) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM; TESCAN, 5136 MM) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS, X-Act, Oxford), respectively, were used to determine the functional groups and the surface morphology observations of CQD/TiO2. An X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific) with a monochromatized Al Kα line (1486.6 eV) was used to perform the surface chemical compositions of CQD/TiO2. The photoluminescence (PL, Cermax Brcllle) emission spectra of the samples were measured to investigate the recombination of electrons/holes in CQD/TiO2. The nano-size and/or atomic level of the photocatalysts were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; HITACHI, HT7700).

As depicted in Fig. 1, the photocatalytic activities by illumination under light source (254 nm lamp; Philips) and the light emitting diode lamps (LED lamps) were detected by measuring the decomposition rate of acetone. The LED lamps provided light of 365 nm LED, 500 nm LED, and 700 nm LED. The wavelength distribution and light intensity on the external surface of the inner reactor tube were analyzed by an LI-COR LI-1800 (LI-COR). The samples were detected for the acetone concentration with a Thermo TVA 1000 (Kaoten) equipped with an FID detector, and the temperature of the photoreactor was controlled at 25 °C for all experiments.

To identify the performance of CQDs/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane as a medical mask material, the particle filtration efficiency (PFE) test and the test of differential pressure were conducted following the ASTM Standards (ASTM F2299) and European Standard (EN 14683), respectively. The filter quality of 16 cm2 CQDs/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane could be evaluated appropriately by differential pressure and penetration parameters using the equation (Eq. 1).
(1)
Q
f
=
(
− l n P
)
/ Δ P
where ΔP, P, and Q
f represented the differential pressure across the filter (Pa), the particle penetration, and filter quality (Pa−1), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of CQD, CQD/TiO2, and CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membranes
Crystal phase analysis via X-ray diffractometer
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A and B show that the synthesized CQD solution appeared color under UV emission because of the blue light shift effect. The appearance of synthesized CQD and the synthesized CQD acquires a high energy gap, while the particle size is small because of the quantum confinement effect. Fig. 2C depicts the appearance of synthesized CQD/TiO2 by TEM, and the particle size of CQD/TiO2 was determined in the ranges of 17 to 20 nm using ImageJ software.

After the synthesis of CQD/TiO2 was successful, CQD/TiO2 was coated upon PI/PES membrane to generate CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane. An SEM with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy EDS (SEM/EDS) was also employed to analyze the presence of CQD/TiO2 and loaded on PI/PES for confirming the synthesized materials as CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane. EDS analysis could identify the elements Ti, O, Au, C, and S in the synthesized CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES, as shown in Fig. 2D and Fig. 2E, while the element S was originally present in the structure of PES. The minimizing chart in Fig. 2D and Fig. 2E present the distribution of titanium and carbon atoms on the surface of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal phases analysis via X-ray diffraction spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Three crystal phases of TiO2were observed, including rutile (R), anatase (A), and brookite; anatase acquired the optimal photocatalytic activity. Fig. 3 reveals a typical set of XRD spectra for TiO2 (P25), TiO2, and a series of CQD/ TiO2 photocatalysts during the analysis of the crystal phases. XRD spectra revealed two crystal phases of TiO2 (P25), including (R) and (A), while anatase acquired the optimal photocatalytic activity. XRD results showed the main peaks of anatase at 2θ= 25.28°(101), 37.80°(004), 48.05°(200), and 53.89°(105), as shown in the samples of TiO2, 3% QD/TiO2, 5% QD/TiO2, and 7% QD/TiO2, but the peaks of rutile did not appear in the samples. The crystal phases of a series of CQD/ TiO2 determined from the XRD spectra can be mainly classified into the anatase component without any additional CQD peaks. Therefore, the addition of CQD on synthesized TiO2 did not generate any new specific peaks; that is, the addition of CQD did not alter the crystal phase of TiO2. An earlier research [24] proposed that the synthesized CQD/TiO2 also does not acquire the peaks of the crystal phase of carbon, which was similar to the result of this study.

The XPS spectra of C 1 s ( Fig. 4A), Ti 2p (Fig. 4B), and O 1 s (Fig. 4C) core levels in the TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 were analyzed to assess the change in surface chemistry between CQD and TiO2. The peaks at binding energies of 284 eV, 285 eV, and 288 eV depicted the chemical bonding states of C-C, CC, and C-O, and these peaks were mainly attributed to sp2 orbit. After CQD was loaded on TiO2, the chemical bonding states of 284.57 eV shifted to 284.53 eV, 284.31 eV, and 284.41 eV due to the CQD doping for the decrease in C-C bonding energy. The research [16] also proposed that the bonding energy reasonably reduced in the synthesized CQDs decorated TiO2 for the acetaldehyde removal.

The XPS spectra (Fig. 4B) revealed the narrow scans for Ti 2p peaks of the TiO2 located at 464.6 eV (Ti 2p1/2) and 458.8 eV (Ti 2p3/2). The two orbital domains peaks of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 for 3% CQD/TiO2, 5% CQD/TiO2, and 7% CQD/TiO2 appeared at 458.26 eV, 458.17 eV, 458.20 eV and 464.01 eV, 463.95 eV, 463.85 eV, respectively. Compared with the results of TiO2 at Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 peaks, the results of CQD/TiO2 shifted toward lower binding energy. The lower binding energy reflected the increase in the number of electrons in the corresponding electron orbitals. The binding energies of 3% CQD/TiO2, 5% CQD/TiO2, and 7% CQD/TiO2 at Ti 2p peak and Ti-O-Ti at O 1 s peaks were lower than that of TiO2, indicating that TiO2loaded with CQDs acquired more electrons than pure TiO2, in line with the outcome of an earlier study [16]. This phenomenon expressed that the electron was shifted from TiO2 to CQD, which derived the electron at the surface redistributed, and light caused the electron-hole effect, then accumulating at the side of carbon quantum, as corresponded to the result of the study [16]. Moreover, Fig. 4C shows the decrease in the binding energy of TiO2 because carbon quantum is smoothly loaded on the surface of TiO2 via the Ti-O-C bond as the result of [25].

Fig. 5A displays the PL emission of CQDs using different excitation wavelengths from 290 to 440 nm. The PL emission peaks of CQD shifted to a longer wavelength and exhibited a clear up-conversion property as the excitation wavelength changed. In addition, CQD indicated an excitation-dependent and a down-conversion PL behavior.

As shown in Fig. 5B, the up-converted PL emission peaks exhibited a red shift with stronger intensity from 300 to 450 nm, increasing excitation wavelengths from 600 to 800 nm with one peak per 50 nm. Moreover, the energy gap of anatase is 3.2 eV, so ultraviolet with a wavelength less than 380 nm can excite TiO2. Furthermore, PL emission of CQD presented that CQD converted visible light and infrared light to shorter wavelengths to excite TiO2. After electrons of CQD in the π orbital were excited by long-wavelength (low-energy) photons, the photo-generated electrons translate to the high-energy state (π*), and then the electrons (π*) fall to the level of σ orbital for emitting a shorter wavelength light by radiative relaxation. The author [26] proposed the synthesis of graphite CQDs via the electrochemical method, which could transform up-conversion wavelength to the range of 330–530 nm due to a two-photon process. Moreover, the author [27] investigated the synthesis of quantum dots/TiO2 by using L-ascorbic acid and titanium isopropoxide, which could emit the stimulated light with a wavelength ranging from 355 to 525 nm while up-conversion wavelength transformed to the range from 600 to 800 nm.

Fig. 5C shows the order of PL intensity of the synthesized sample, which ranged as TiO2, 3% CQD/TiO2, 7% CQD/TiO2, and 5% CQD/TiO2 from high to low PL intensity, while the testing wavelength is set as 310 nm. The peak of the PL spectrum tended to decrease as the loading of CQDs increased, but the PL spectrum intensity increased when the loading of CQDs increased to 7%. The experimental results revealed that the 5% CQD loading acquired a smaller PL intensity and lower recombination of photogenerated electron-holes to improve the photocatalytic activity than that of a sample with different CQD loading. In the experimental results of various contents of CQDs, the PL intensity depicted a significant decay with the increase of CQDs contents, compared with pure TiO2 [28,29]. The research [28] proposed that the loading of CQDs indicated rapid charge transfer and trapping of electrons at defect sites, and 6% CQDs loading acquired the lowest PL intensity.

The possible mechanism of CQD operation was simulated and plotted based on results of the proof of PL measurements (Fig. 5D). CQD could absorb visible light and near-infrared light, which were converted to shorter wavelengths of less than or equal to 380 nm due to up-conversion properties, allowing more efficient use of sunlight. With the loading of CQD, the PL intensity of TiO2 was significantly reduced, indicating that CQD could accept photo-generated electrons from TiO2 and then promote the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the excess electrons could activate the oxygen (O2•) adsorbed on the surface of the composite material to produce superoxide radical anions (O2 -), and the holes could react with water to form reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH), thereby degrading pollutants [30,31]. Thus, CQD acquired a dual mechanism to enhance the photovoltaic performance of TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of photocatalytic degradation at different experimental conditions by the Taguchi method
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation of nanofiber membrane via the Taguchi method (the larger, the better) was used to analyze photocatalysis efficiency of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES nanofiber membrane. This study considered four factors: PI/PES, contents of CQD/TiO2, operational voltage, and electrospinning time used to obtain the best photocatalytic degradation. Every factor involved three levels to observe the variations of photocatalysis efficiency at different combinations of factors.

The contents included PI/PES (38 wt%, 40 wt%, and 42 wt%), CQD/TiO2 (3 wt%, 5 wt%, and 7 wt%), the operational voltage (20.0 kV, 22.5 kV, and 25.0 kV), and the electrospinning time (2 h, 4 h, and 6 h). The signal-to-noise (S/N) value determined photocatalytic degradation, while the variation of S/N value depicted the strength and weakness of photocatalytic degradation during the analysis of various combinations of factors, and the great photocatalytic degradation acquired a high S/N value. Accordingly, the condition of the larger, was better for analyzing photocatalytic degradation, and the Eq. (2) (Eq. 2) depicted the Taguchi condition of ‘the larger, the better’. Table 1 presents different S/N values at various combinations of factors as the indicator of the membrane’s photocatalytic degradation. The combinations of factors were designed as three classes of PI/PES concentrations (38 wt%, 40 wt%, and 42 wt%), and the other three factors were randomly arranged. As a result, the calculation of L9 (34) was applied to analyze the S/N values of the combinations of factors.
(2)
S / N = − 10 × log
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
1
y
i
2

Table 2 shows the factor influence order, which was arranged as PI/PES, electrospinning time, contents of CQD/TiO2, and operational voltage according to its factor values of 2.23, 1.89, 1.46, and 0.32, respectively. Thus, the contents of PI/PES were the most influential factor for the photocatalytic degradation of the CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES nanofiber membrane. In these experimental results at a 300-ppm initial concentration of acetone, the optimal experimental conditions for the best photocatalytic degradation, at 74.8%, under UV light illumination were 42 wt% PI/PES, 5 wt% CQD/TiO2, 20.0 kV voltage, and 6 h of collection time (L8).

As a result, larger contents of PI/PES (42 wt%) could produce a membrane with the best photocatalytic degradation if the target goal was photocatalytic degradation (the larger, the better). Photocatalytic degradation increased with an increase in the electrospinning time; this may be because the load of CQD/TiO2 on the nanofiber membrane increased with electrospinning time. In contrast, the load of CQD/TiO2 could enhance the concentration of active site and the surface area. However, when the content of CQD/TiO2 addition reached to more than 5%, it resulted in the decline of photocatalytic degradation because of poor light penetration, increased light scattering effect, and particle accumulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factor influencing the content of PI/PES on the fiber diameter, viscosity, thickness, and photocatalytic degradation of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As the content of PI/PES increased, the viscosity of the electrospinning solution also increased ( Table 3). A lesser content of PI/PES (≤38%) and viscosity (≤2400 cP) loosened the molecular linking during the electrospinning process, resulting the formation of in many droplets in CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane ( Fig. 6A) and an unstable jet of electrospinning from the spray nozzle. Fig. 6B reveals the same result as Fig. 6A, where the uniformity of the fiber diameter deteriorated to coat CQD/TiO2 in the polymer by the formed droplets. As shown in Table 3, the contacting probability between CQD/TiO2 and VOCs was too less to decrease the photocatalytic degradation and fiber diameter due to the cover of the active site by PI/PES polymer.

As depicted in Fig. 6C, Fig. 6D, and Table 3, a larger content of PI/PES (≥40%) and viscosity (≤2400 cP) generated a more uniform and larger fiber diameter without droplets through the tractive force of the electronic field owing to the high degree of entanglement between molecular linking during the electrospinning process. Accordingly, when the content of PI/PES was adjusted to 42 wt%, the photocatalytic degradation reached the maximum value of 76.5% because of more active sites on thicker nanofibers. The possible reason is that the thicker membrane, can make a wider and more uniform distribution of CQD/TiO2 for enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Based on the best condition (42 wt% PI/PES, 5 wt% CQD/TiO2, 20.0 kV voltage, and 6 h collection time), above 99% particle filtration efficiency (PFE) was achieved with the lowest differential pressure (ΔP=0.34 Pa), accompanied with the best filtration quality of 0.151 Pa−1. According to the medical face masks standards of ASTM F2299 and EN 14683 [33], CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membranes can be suitably utilized as a potential material for creating level 3 medical face masks (PFE>98% and ΔP＜58.8 Pa/cm2). CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane could filtrate particles from 10 to 500 nm and decompose acetone by the photocatalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQD/TiO2 contents on photocatalytic degradation and filter efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Various CQD/TiO2 contents were applied on the PI/PES membrane to examine photocatalytic degradation and filtration efficiency, as shown in Table 4 The reference condition of the CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane in the electrospinning process was 40 wt% PI/PES, 5 wt% CQD loading of TiO2, 20.0 kV, and CQD/TiO2 contents on PI/PES membrane varied as 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt%, and 7 wt%. The photocatalytic degradation of acetone increased as contents of CQD/TiO2 on PI/PES membrane increased from 1 wt% to 5 wt% with a constant CQD (5 wt%) loading on TiO2. However, the photocatalytic degradation of acetone decreased for the experiment conducted with 7% CQD/TiO2 on the PI/PES membrane. Excess contents of CQD/TiO2 on the surface of PI/PES membrane resulted in the aggregation of CQD/TiO2 particles, hindered light delivery, and increased light scattering, while the photocatalytic degradation of acetone was not found to increase.

The same trend was noted for the surface electric potential of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES, filter efficiency, and filter quality at various contents of CQD/TiO2 on PI/PES membrane. Different CQD/TiO2 contents caused variation in CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES electric potential of the membrane surface, and the filtration mechanism changed from the initial conditions of interception, inertial impaction, and diffusion to a mechanism involving electrostatic attraction. In Fig. 6D, Fig. 6E, and Fig. 7, the particles of sodium chloride in the range of 10 to 500 nm were easily attracted on CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane with positive charges. and if the particle diameter was small, the morphology of fiber membrane after filtration depicted electrostatic attraction acquired obvious effect. Earlier research [32] also revealed that electrostatic attraction could attract or repel many particles when the particle size was small.

The surface electric potential of 1 wt% CQD/TiO2 content on the @PI/PES membrane acquired the smallest value of 0.30 kV. As a result, the filtration efficiency of 1 wt% CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane for the removal of ultrafine NaCl particles of size in the range of 10 to 500 nm only reached 98.86%, because it could not effectively filtrate the small size of NaCl particles from 10 to 100 nm. Furthermore, as CQD/TiO2 contents increased from 1 wt% to 5 wt%, the filtration efficiency and quality increased due to the enhancement of the fiber membrane’s surface electric potential and electrostatic attraction for the attachment of size particles. Moreover, the addition of metal nanoparticles could enhance the potential of the membrane, so the addition of the TiO2 to fiber membrane caused the interaction of electric charge on the fiber membrane to enhance its surface potential, which could affect the strength of electrostatic attraction during filtration process of micro and nanoparticles [34]. When CQD/TiO2 contents (7 wt%) were exceeded, CQD/TiO2 occurred polarization phenomenon on the electric charge neutralized positive and negative electric charge, resulting in the decrease of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane’s surface electric potential to influence its filtration efficiency and filtration quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of carbon quantum dot loading on photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para: This study employed different CQD loading on TiO2 of PI/PES membrane (42%PI/PES, 5% CQD/TiO2, 20 kV, and 6 h) to conduct photocatalyst tests of 150 ppm acetone under UV light illumination to obtain the results presented in Fig. 8. The synthesized CQDTiO2 @PI/PES membrane could degrade 73.4% of acetone with 1% CQD loading, and the degradation of acetone rose from 73.4% to 94.5% after 5% CQD loading. The synthesized CQDs/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane with 5% CQDs loading presented the best photocatalytic degradation, about 2.4 times as high as TiO2(P25)@PI/PES. However, the CQD loading increased to 7% to result in the degradation of acetone reduced to 69.7% because 7% CQD/TiO2 generated higher photo intensity via photoluminescence analysis accompanied with recombination of electron and hole reaction than that of 3% and 5% CQD/TiO2 (Fig. 5C). Shafiqu et al. proposed that 10% CQD loading on TiO2 presented the optimal photocatalyst efficiency for methyl orange dye, compared with results of exceeding CQD loading cover on the TiO2 surface to hindering the photon collection, photoelectron transition, and reduction of photocatalyst efficiency [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of light wavelength on photocatalytic degradation of acetone
ce:section.ce:para: The visible photocatalytic activity of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane with 5 wt% CQD/TiO2 was identified by the decomposition rate of acetone under 365 nm UV LED, 500 nm green LED, and 700 nm red LED illumination ( Fig. 9). Acetone could not convert pure TiO2 under 500 nm LED irradiation, but 5 wt% CQD/TiO2 could extend the visible light absorption range from 365 nm to 700 nm. For 500 and 700 nm LED illumination, decomposition of 45% after a reaction time of 5 h was obtained with a 0.50 wt% CQD/TiO2; furthermore, decomposition of more than 75% was obtained for 365 nm LED illumination. Doping TiO2 with CQD can serve as a photoelectron mediator and extend the visible light absorption range to the long wavelength region. The decomposition of acetone under LED illumination increased with the decrease in wavelength of LEDs owing to the higher energy of the photons. This study presented similar results with that reported earlier for ZnO and TiO2 [36]. Recently, one research [24] proposed that CQD loaded on TiO2 could improve the photocatalytic activity for the degradation rate of VOCs due to the formation of quantum-scale reaction regions and the enhancement of the charge separation of TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic calculation of CQD loading on photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of light intensity on the photodegradation of acetone is shown in Fig. 10A. The photodegradation of acetone by CQD/TiO2 was increased with the increase in light intensity. The results of the isothermal heterogeneous photocatalytic reaction of acetone by CQD/TiO2 can be correlated using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood rate equation (Eq. 3):
(3)
−
∂
C
A
∂ t
= −
r
A
= k ⋅
I
max
m
(
K
ads
⋅
C
A
1 +
K
ads
⋅
C
A
)
where C
A is the concentration of acetone (ppmv), r
A is photocatalytic rate of acetone (ppmv·h−1), t is the reaction time (h), I
max is the incident light intensity (mW·m−2), m is the reaction order of light intensity, k is the intrinsic reaction rate constant (ppmv·h−1), and K
ads is the adsorption equilibrium constant of acetone (ppmv−1). Attributed to the quite low initial concentrations of acetone, the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic equation (Eq. 4) could be integrated for a homogeneous batch photoreactor as:
(4)
l n

A
/

)

K

–

A 0
)

)

a d s
t

With the results at various initial concentrations of acetone, the adsorption equilibrium constant of acetone (K
ads ) was determined to be 0.002 ppmv−1 by plotting the ln (C
A
/C
A0
) versus (C
A
−C
A0
) for experiments conducted at ﬁxed light intensity (Fig. 10 A). The above equation was rearranged to determine the values of m and k by correlating the ln {− [ln(C
A
/C
A0
)+K
ads
(C
A
−C
A0
)]} versus ln (I
max
) for experiments conducted at various light intensities as follows:
(5)

)
]

)

K

)

In the plot of Eq. (5) depicted in Fig. 10B, the reaction order of light intensity (m) and the intrinsic reaction rate constant (k) were determined to be 0.66 and 5.185 × 10−4 ppmv·h−1, respectively, for acetone degradation. Hence, the experimental results for the photocatalytic acetone degradation by CQD/TiO2 can be correlated by the following rate equation (Eq. 6):
(6)
−
r
A
= 5.185 ⋅ 1
0
- 4
⋅
I
max
0.66
⋅
0.002 ⋅
C
A
1 + 0.002 ⋅
C
A

The relevant kinetics results in this study showed that the reaction order for acetone was from 0.5 to 1, consistent with the outcome inferred by the photodegradation mechanism reasonably. The researchers [37,38] have proposed that calculated reaction orders by Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetics were nearly 0.5 at a low light intensity and approached unity at high light intensity. The reaction orders model of 0.5 fits the acetone conversion data using a non-circulating tubular reactor; the reaction rate constant of acetone was 3.3 × 10−5 mol L−1 s−1 [39]. For the photo-oxidation of methylene blue, the reaction order for light intensity was equal to 1 [37].

Comparison of photodegradation and filtration results at various photocatalysts and filter membranes are presented in Table 5. All filter membranes exhibited more than 95% filtration efficiency accompanied with great filtration quality for PM0.3 and PM0.5 removals. However, compared with other filter materials, CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane in this study acquired the lowest differential pressure for energy saving in practical applications and the most effective ultrafine particles (nano-size of particles from 10 to 100 nm) removal, simultaneously degrading acetone photo-catalytically in the gaseous phase.

Using the Taguchi experimental design, the analysis of the S/N value indicated that the concentrations of PI/PES were the most operational parameter of influence on the photocatalytic degradation of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES electrospinning nanofibers. An effective, smooth, and uniformly CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES nanofiber membranes were successfully synthesized at the optimal parameter of a 42 wt% PI/PES, 5 wt% CQD/TiO2, 20.0 kV voltage, and 6 h of collection time. Following the standards of EN 14683 and ASTM F2299, the CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES nanofiber membrane exhibited the highest particle filtration efficiency of 99.9% and the lowest differential pressure of 0.34 Pa with the filter quality of 0.151 Pa−1, effectively filtering ultrafine particles. The CQDs/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane with 5% CQDs loading exhibited the best photocatalytic activity, about 2.4 times as high as that of TiO2(P25)@PI/PES. The enhancement of visible light absorption, up-conversion properties of CQD, and the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs were possible mechanisms that improve the photovoltaic performance of TiO2. Moreover, recycled test of CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES membrane presented approximate 89% photocatalytic degradation of acetone for first-and second-times recycled test at time of 11 hr and 17 hr, as shown in Appendix Fig. A. According to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic equation, the reaction order of light intensity and the intrinsic reaction rate constant for the degradation of acetone by CQDs/TiO2 @PI/PES membranes were determined to be 0.66 and 5.185 × 10−4 ppmv·h−1, respectively. Furthermore, photocatalytic degradation of isopropyl alcohol (IPA) was conducted to test membrane’s VOCs degradation, and 83% of IPA could be degraded under the conditions of 365 nm wavelength, 150 ppm concentration, and 5 hrs operation, as shown in Appendix Fig. B. Additionally, CQD was benefit to photocatalytic degradation at wavelength of visible light (over 400 nm) due to the comparison of the intensity of CQD/TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 @PI/PES, as depicted in Appendix Fig. C. To conclude, dual-functional CQDs/TiO2 @PI/PES membranes possess some advantages, such as charge separation, the energy of light transforms from low energy to high energy, the utilization of visible light, low airflow resistance, small-sized fiber aperture, multiple porosities, high surface area, and nanosized and uniformed size. Thus, membranes simultaneously enhance the high filtration efficiency of ultrafine particles and photocatalytic activity through the via electrospinning process.

Chen Hua-Wei: Writing – original draft. Chen Wei-Ting: Writing – review & editing. Chen Ying-Cih: Project administration. Chiou Chyow-San: Investigation. Huang Shu-Hsien: Investigation. Kuo Yu-Lin: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Wei-Ting Chen reports writing assistance was provided by St. Mary’s Junior College of Medical, nursing and management. Wei-Ting Chen reports writing assistance was provided by St Mary’s Junior College of Medicine Nursing and Management. Wei-Ting Chen reports a relationship with St Mary’s Junior College of Medicine Nursing and Management that includes: employment. Yu-Lin Kuo and Hua-Wei Chen contributed to original draft preparation and writing. Shu-Hsien Huang and Chyow-San Chiou contributed to survey the conception of original draft. Ying-Cih Chen undertook formal analysis and data curation.

see appendix
Figs. A–C.
